data.title: Tamarack Valley: High Profit Margin, But Wading Through Too Many Risks
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant Tamarack Valley a hold rating (OTCPK:TNEYF) (TSX:TVE:CA). I am of the opinion that the company could face significant risks that would affect its operations. The first risk is lower oil prices. The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) projects that the Brent oil price will average $51 per barrel in 2026. In addition, the WTI forward curve indicates that market participants are taking long and short positions in WTI oil futures contracts at prices per barrel in the range of $61 to $64 for the upcoming years. Based on these values, I projected Tamarack's earnings before taxes (EBT), suggesting that it will reach CA$138 million in 2029. This value is 34.15% lower than the EBT reported by the company in 2024. Secondly, the company had a reserve life index of 4.83 years, considerably lower than many of its peers.

However, Tamarack is a profitable firm. It has higher profit margins than many of its peers, since it produces heavy and light oils. In addition, its proven reserves increased by 126.20% from 2018 to 2024. Further, it has reduced its debt level, improving this aspect. Despite considering all the positive aspects, I still rate Tamarack with a hold.

The company

Tamarack is a Canadian oil and gas company headquartered in Calgary, Alberta. The company has transformed its operations. In 2020, most of its activities were concentrated in Cardium and Viking. Meanwhile, in H1 2025, Tamarack's operations were concentrated in Clearwater and Charlie Lake. In the first location, it mainly produced heavy oil, while in the latter location, it focused on producing light oil. During H1 2025, Tamarack averaged a production of 68,986 barrels of oil equivalent per day (boe/d), of which 60% was heavy oil, 21% light oil, and 19% natural gas and NGL, as depicted in the following chart.

Positive Aspects

I divided this article into two main sections. The first one, which is this one, comprises the main positive aspects of the company. Meanwhile, the second main section will focus on some of the challenges that Tamarack faces.

Heavy and Light Oil

Tamarack concentrates its operations on producing light and heavy oil. Due to this characteristic, the company has higher gross profit margins than many other Canadian oil and gas companies. With that being said, the following table shows some profit ratios of Tamarack, Athabasca Oil (OTCPK:ATHOF), Baytex Energy (BTE), Cardinal Energy (OTCPK:CRLFF), Headwater Exploration (OTCPK:CDDRF), International Petroleum (OTCPK:IPCFF), Obsidian Energy (OBE), Surge Energy (OTCPK:ZPTAF), Vermilion Energy (VET), and Whitecap Resources (OTCPK:WCPRF). The company mentions these companies as its peers. As the green cells indicate, Tamarack has the highest gross profit, EBITDA, and levered FCF margins among its peers. In addition, its EBITDA and net income margins are higher than their respective median values.

Many companies in the table above produce bitumen, which is a type of extra-heavy oil. To be transported through pipelines, this type of oil is blended with lighter oil. However, the diluents represent a significant portion of the costs. For instance, ATHOF's costs of diluent accounted for 35.70% of its revenues in 2024. Meanwhile, in the same year, Tamarack reported product purchases and blending (diluents) for CA$19.51 million, representing 1.45% of its revenues. For this reason, Tamarack has a higher profit margin, to some extent.

Breakeven Price

In Q2 2025, the WTI oil price averaged $64.57 per barrel. That quarter, the company reported a net income before taxes margin of 33.13%. To add another perspective, the company indicates that it can cover its costs and pay dividends even if the WTI oil price is below $40 per barrel.

In a report of the EIA, oil producers in the Permian Midland Basin had a breakeven price of $62 per barrel in 2024. Meanwhile, in the Permian Delaware Basin, the breakeven price was $64 per barrel. These are the two largest basins in the Permian. The Permian Basin accounted for 48% of the U.S. oil production in 2024. Additionally, in the U.S., most of the growth in oil and natural gas production is expected to come from the Permian Basin. As such, if oil prices decline below $60, many oil producers in the Permian Basin will reduce their oil production or reduce their capex. Consequently, if the previous scenario occurs, it will reduce the global oil supply, which will ultimately push oil prices up. As such, Tamarack will be in a better position than many oil producers in the Permian Basin.

Oil Reserves

I think that an excellent indication of any oil and gas company is its proved reserves. The more reserves a company has, the more years it can extract oil. The following table shows that Tamarack had 50 million barrels of oil equivalent (MMboe) of net proved reserves in 2018, increasing to 113 MMboe in 2024, representing a 126.20% increase. As such, the company is in a better position.

.

Tamarack has expanded its operations through mergers and acquisitions. In 2020, it completed two strategic acquisitions in Clearwater. With these transactions, the company increased its production by 2,000 boe/d.

In 2022, the company acquired Deltastream Energy Corporation, adding 13.4 MMboe of proved developed reserves and 51.0 MMboe of proved and probable reserves to its operations. The assets are located in Clearwater. In addition, in Q2 2025, Tamarack acquired assets in Clearwater for CA$51.5 million, adding more than 114 net sections of Clearwater lands.

Debt

There are companies with wonderful businesses, but they often experience financial distress or bankruptcy due to inadequate debt management. As such, I always try to include a small section to evaluate the debt position of my write-up.

In Q2 2025, Tamarack only had CA$2.76 million of cash and cash equivalents, and CA$174.04 million of current assets. In the same period, it reported 172.47 in current liabilities. As such, its current liabilities and current assets are similar. However, the company had CA$717.40 million in long-term debt, considerably higher than its current assets.

To reduce its debt, the company sold non-core assets in the Veteran Consort and Eyehill areas, located in Eastern Alberta, Canada. This operation is for CA$112 million before closing adjustments. With these resources, the company expects to reduce its debt. Tamarack expects to lower its debt to CA$500 million by 2027, which is a promising prospect.

Negative Aspects

Up to this point, I have discussed only the positive aspects of Tamarack. However, this article will be incomplete if I do not discuss some of the potential risks that could undermine Tamarack's operations.

Energy Outlook and Projection

The EIA expects lower oil prices for 2025 and 2026 compared to 2024, which is negative for Tamarack. The institution projects that the Brent oil price will average $67 per barrel in 2025 and $51 per barrel in 2026, as depicted in the following chart. If the Brent oil price declines to $51 in 2026, Tamarack could face a similar situation to the one observed in 2016 and 2017.

The company's performance is closely tied to oil production, since most of Tamarack's revenues are derived from oil. In its 2025 Investor Day, Tamarack projects that the WTI oil price will average $75 for 2025-2029. However, as the previous chart indicates, the EIA projections suggest lower oil prices for this year and 2026. To add context, the Brent oil price tends to trade at a higher price than the WTI oil price. As such, the EIA projections point out that the WTI oil price would average less than $51 per barrel in 2026. For projection purposes, I will assume that the WTI oil price will average $49 in 2026, which is $2 lower than the Brent oil price.

For 2027-2029, I anticipate that the WTI will average $62 per barrel. This value is taken from the WTI forward curve, as depicted below. The forward curve shows the future market perspective of the oil price. The chart indicates that market participants are taking long and short positions in the range of $61-$64 per barrel of WTI futures contracts for the upcoming years. Since the EIA only implements projections for this year and the next, I think that using the WTI forward curve to project the WTI oil price is a reasonable assumption.

Hitherto, I have only discussed the WTI oil price. Albeit I have not projected Tamarack's oil production. As such, the following paragraph of this section will project the production and revenues of the company.

For this year, Tamarack expects to produce between 65,000 boe/d and 67,000 boe/d. At the midpoint of this expectation, production is expected to increase by 2.59% annually in 2025. For 2029, the company expects to produce approximately between 75,000 and 80,000 boe/d (page 6 of Tamarack's June 2025 investor day presentation). Assuming that the company produces 75,000 boe/d in 2029, it will represent a 16.58% increase compared to 2024. I use 75,000 boe/d, the lower band, to be conservative with my projection.

With the projection presented in the previous paragraph, it is possible to derive Tamarack's growth outlook. Assuming that Tamarack's revenues per boe will move in the same proportion as the WTI, the revenues of Tamarack will be the sum of the expected oil price growth and the company's production growth, as depicted in the following table. For instance, in 2025, my projection assumes that Tamarack's revenues will decline by 8.52%. This rate is the sum of the expected production growth and the expected oil price growth for that year (2.59% - 11.11% = -8.52%). The estimation suggests that the company's revenues would be CA$933 million in 2026. Meanwhile, according to my forecast for 2029, the revenues are expected to be CA$1,321 million.

Estimation

This section is the continuation of the previous section. I separated the previous section from this one to facilitate the comprehension of the article.

As depicted in the following table, from 2022 to the first half of 2025 (H1 2025), the total costs before taxes that Tamarack incurred to produce a boe have fluctuated from CA$39 to CA$49. In H1 2025, these costs were CA$41.81 per boe. I will assume that these costs will remain constant at CA$41.81 per boe for 2025-2029. Note that the previous figure is closer to CA$39 (the lower band). As such, my projection could overestimate Tamarack's costs.

With the revenues per boe (projected in the previous section) and the total costs before taxes per boe, it is possible to project the EBT of the company per boe. For 2026, my estimation suggests that Tamarack will generate a negative EBT of 4.61 per boe, as depicted in the table below. I assumed that the WTI would average $49 per barrel in 2026. For that reason, the company is projected to generate financial losses that year. The EBT for the full year (2026) is expected to be a loss of CA$116 million. Meanwhile, the company is expected to generate an EBT of CA$138 million in 2029. This value is 34.15% lower than the EBT reported by the company in 2024.

From 2024 to 2029E, my estimations suggest that Tamarack would reach an EBT of CA$711 million. The current market capitalization is approximately CA$2,920 million. Consequently, due to the expected low oil prices, the expansion plan does not seem to be promising for the company.

Reserve Life Index

Despite the fact that Tamarack has increased its oil reserves, its reserve life index is still relatively low. In 2024, the company had 113,373 Mboe of proved reserves, and a reserve life index of 4.83 years. In other words, the company will have to invest significant resources in exploration and acquisition of new assets to sustain its production.

There are Canadian oil companies with a higher life index than Tamarack. For instance, Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) had a total proved reserve life index of 33 years in 2024. That year, WCPRF reported a total proved reserve life index of 12.5 years. Considering that the company has virtually no liquid assets, long-term debt of CA$717.40 million, and expects to reduce its long-term debt to CA$500 million, I think the company will have difficulties acquiring new assets in the short term.

Peer Analysis

Up to this point, I have compared Tamarack's profitability ratios to those of its peers. However, I have not included other critical metrics. This section conducts a peer analysis, including other crucial metrics, among Tamarack, ATHOF, BTE, CRLFF, CDDRF, IPCFF, OBE, ZPTAF, VET, and WCPRF. The following table shows the ratios to analyze.

In the last year, Tamarack's stock price increased by 53.87%, the highest increase among its peers. In addition, its five-year stock return was 747.15%, higher than the median return in that period. Overall, the company has had an excellent performance in the financial market among its peers.

Moving to the valuation ratios, Tamarack has ratios relatively similar to those reported by its peers. For example, it has a 1.37 price-to-book ratio, while the median price-to-book ratio is 1.245. The price-to-cash flow ratio is the only metric that suggests a considerable undervaluation.

On the other hand, the growth ratios indicate that Tamarack will face headwinds for this year. The analyst consensus expects that the company's revenues will decrease by 3.84%, lower than the median ratio. Nonetheless, the company achieved a 40.50% CAGR in its revenues over the last five years, which is an excellent value.

Finally, the debt ratio suggests that the company has achieved results comparable to those reported by its peers. Tamarack has a debt-to-equity of 35.04%, while the median ratio is 32.20%, relatively similar values. Meanwhile, its current ratio is slightly higher than that reported by its peers.

I think that Tamarack exhibits financial ratios relatively similar to those of the median ratios in the peer group. The valuation ratios are similar to their respective median values. The debt ratios of Tamarack are also similar to their respective median values. Therefore, I think that the company should have a similar performance to the theoretical median company in the peer group.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I believe that the company is not likely to experience excellent performance in the upcoming years. As such, I rate the company with a hold rating. Oil prices have declined compared to 2024. At the current price, the company will not generate the same resources that it generated before. My estimation suggests that the EBT will be CA$138 million in 2029, 34.15% lower than the EBT reported by the company in 2024. This estimation assumes a $62 per WTI oil barrel in 2029, and anticipates that the annual oil production will increase by 20.47% from 2024 to 2029E.

A positive aspect is that Tamarack is more profitable than its peers, and it also surpasses many oil companies in the Permian basin. As such, the company does not show symptoms of financial distress. It recently sold some of its non-core assets to reduce its debt level. To conclude, despite some positive aspects, I think that the current low oil price context will affect the company's revenues and earnings. As my projections suggest, the company will not have excellent profit margins for the foreseeable future, supporting my hold rating

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - October 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 (SP500) pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who Should Follow This Series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

The market has been making new all-time highs on the heels of the Fed's first rate cut in almost a year and expectations of a couple more rate cuts in 2025. Right now, it seems nothing matters to the market except the interest rates. The U.S. Govt has been in shutdown for nearly two weeks now, but that hasn’t really impacted the market. When President Trump suddenly announced fresh 100% tariffs on China last Friday, markets were shocked for a day, but they recovered most of the losses on the following Monday. So, unless there is really some kind of shock, the sentiment is pretty strong and likely to remain so for some time. The only thing that can upset the sentiment is if the Fed does not deliver on further rate cuts.

As the housing market, as well as the health of the broader economy, will depend greatly on what the Fed will do or not do in the coming months, you can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatch tool website.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link is provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95%, but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score."

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, CI, CTRA, FIX, MLI, RL, and SAN (7 duplicates).

Appeared three times: AVGO, HWM, LRCX (6 duplicates).

We are now left with 37 (50 - 13) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes LEN, OTIS, OVV, CI, CARR, AMH, CTAS, CTRA, DKS, MSCI, and IBN. This leaves us 26 names (37-11).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. There is none. We still have 26 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. We remove TS and FSV. After removing these two names, we are left with 24 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major:

Finance-Regional Banks: We keep SAN and BK but remove ZION.

Financial Services and Misc.: We keep MS and COF but remove VOYA.

Computer Software: We will keep MSFT.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH but remove KLAC.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO and LRCX but remove TSM.

Industrial- Aerospace & Defense: We keep HWM.

Industrial- Const. & Engineering: We keep PH and MLI.

Utility: We keep NFG.

In all, we removed four names at this stage, so we are finally left with 20 (24-4) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(FIX), (SAN), (BK), (MSFT), (TPR), (RL), (EME), (APH), (COF), (MS), (HWM), (HIG), (LLY), (AL), (PH), (RGLD), (MLI), (LRCX), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is "not" a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative Strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Oct. 2025):

(FIX), (SAN), (TPR), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (AL), (RGLD), (MLI), and (AVGO).

Previous Month Selections:

(FIX), (SAN), (RL), (EME), (APH), (HWM), (MLI), (WPM), (AVGO), and (NFG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. We are retaining seven out of 10 stocks from the previous month, and three stocks were replaced with new ones.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

After 8.6%, 8.8%, 12.5%, 5.8%, and 7.1% gains (successively) in the previous five months, the portfolio growth slowed down this past month. But it still outperformed the S&P 500.

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P 500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from January 2012 until Aug. 2025. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 12 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF, and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of January 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

After five months of a very strong showing, the performance this month slowed down quite a bit. The portfolio gained roughly 2.5%, but it was still ahead of the S&P 500. The S&P 500 and our benchmark ETF, VIG, gained 1.06% and 0.75% in the same period, respectively. The star performers for this past month were Comfort Systems USA (FIX) and EMCOR Group (EME). Stocks such as Amphenol (APH), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), and Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) performed reasonably well. However, we are replacing WPM with Royal Gold (RGLD). Also, Ralph Lauren (RL) is being replaced with Tapestry (TPR). We are also introducing/adding Air Lease (AL).

This month, we are retaining seven stocks from last month and only replacing three stocks. The portfolio of 10 stocks is highly diversified into many different sectors and industry segments.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not buy recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially when the market appears to be overvalued. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: It Looks Like A Bubble, It Feels Like A Bubble, But It Isn't
data.description:
Introduction

It’s now 35 years since Howard Marks wrote his first memo, which is a big deal, as his memos have become almost must-read material for Wall Street. This makes sense for a number of reasons, one of which is the fact that Howard Marks has made a fortune as the co-founder of Oaktree and has become a giant in distressed opportunities.

The other reason, as I heard on TV the other day, is that he offers a clear Big Picture view to investors, who are often way too occupied with the daily movements of the market. While I would never compare myself to Howard Marks, that’s what I also try to do, as I believe most investors get carried away in the daily volatility and barrages of macroeconomic and geopolitical headlines.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has gone almost 100 days without a 5% pullback:

The number of "bubble" mentions in news articles has exploded:

Hence, right now, I believe it makes a lot of sense to zoom out a bit, as the market is trading at a very lofty valuation, people don’t know what to expect from the Fed, inflation is still sticky, trade war fears have returned, consumer sentiment is bad, cyclical growth is horrible, and we’re about to enter a year with midterm elections, just to name a few things.

So, as you can imagine, when Howard Marks was interviewed (by CNBC), he was also asked about this market environment. His answer created some headlines as he said the market is expensive but not in a bubble, as fundamental growth in market-leading stocks like the Big Tech AI beneficiaries has done a lot of the heavy lifting:

“To me, the main ingredient in bubbles is psychological excess ... some kind of temporary mania,” he said. “For a company in this sector or industry, there’s no such thing as a price too high. And I don’t detect that level of mania at this time, so I have not put the bubble label on this incident ... it just hasn’t reached that critical mass of mania.” - Via CNBC

I agree with that and believe there’s one way to capitalize on this environment.

It Looks And Smells Like A Bubble. However…

Don’t get me wrong, I truly get people’s concerns when it comes to the durability of the market’s rally. I really do. After all, as I mentioned quite frequently, the market trades at nearly 23.0x earnings. Historically speaking, this implies poor long-term returns:

As I wrote yesterday, this is OK as long as we can find value in areas with secular growth. Think of AI data centers, infrastructure, power suppliers, defense modernization, cyclical value, economic re-shoring, and others.

However, some really take this to the next level, which gives me strong 2021 vibes.

For example, quantum computing stock Rigetti Computing (RGTI) is up more than 270% YTD while I am writing this. It now trades at 2,300x sales. While sales will obviously rise, it’s a great example of a stock that people cannot get enough of just because it operates in an industry with tremendous long-term potential.

Meanwhile, Rocket Lab (RKLB), a company I have been super bullish on in the past. While I am very happy it’s working out much better than I expected, I have to say that this rally is quite something, as it’s now a $33 billion company trading at nearly 70x sales.

And then there are companies like Nebius (NBIS), which is up nearly 400% year-to-date, fueled by a multi-billion dollar deal with Microsoft (MSFT), as hyperscalers are desperate for computing power in the race for AI superiority.

In general, when it comes to AI, the market is somewhat skeptical, as many of the same major AI players are closing deals with each other. As we can see below, there is certainly a circular deal structure going on, where everyone invests in each other, including buyers and their suppliers.

Here’s what that looks like when we visualize it:

If you see a new circular announcement almost every day and certain stocks rally 20-30% per week just because they announced some great news in an industry with great potential, it sure feels like a bubble.

However, while we should certainly stay vigilant, these deals are not as bad as they may look. For example, it’s normal for big players to grow along with each other. For the same reason, large car producers tend to have stakes in smaller ones, and do large defense contractors buy large stakes in small companies to benefit from their innovation? L3Harris Technologies (LHX) is one of these companies. They invest in both customers and suppliers to create a strong network and de-risk their own business models.

This would become a problem if the hyperscalers were to run into a situation where AI demand flatlines for whatever reason. At that point, even if they wanted to (hypothetical situation), mislead investors, they would require circular deals that are simply not possible.

The Wall Street Journal seems to agree with my thesis, as it notes that as long as demand is healthy (which is certainly the case), the AI thesis itself isn’t a bubble:

That demand will only continue to soar. OpenAI’s new Sora short-form AI video app powered by the latest version of its Sora video generator is a hit. (News Corp has a content-licensing partnership with OpenAI.) The company is looking to ramp up efforts in AI commerce as well. And Anthropic’s new large language model, Claude Sonnet 4.5, can code for 30 hours straight. Such longer-thinking models are an emerging form of AI that will put the industry’s ballooning data-center capacity to use.

AI startups are losing tens of billions of dollars more than they would have partly because of this dynamic. Startups and venture-capital investors so far are underwriting such losses, much as Uber Technologies once subsidized ride prices for users to build a market, Terry said. - WSJ

Now, that doesn’t mean we should just keep pushing every single AI-related company into the stratosphere. Valuations still very much matter, as do cash flows and actual business developments. We cannot just go out and buy everything related to this booming trend.

That’s exactly why I believe the market is not in a bubble.

  • The market will decide who wins the AI race and who doesn’t. So far, none of these hyper-speculative stocks has actually driven the market’s returns.
  • While Big Tech has basically turned the S&P 500 into an AI ETF, as I sometimes jokingly say, and they are increasingly competing with each other, it merely weakens the risk/reward of the market. It doesn’t create a bubble, as every single Big Tech leader in the market is growing at a fast clip with valuations that can be justified if we’re even somewhat bullish on AI disruption.

Again, I’m not saying the market is cheap and that all the fast-growing examples I used (RKLB, etc.) are doomed to crater. I’m saying that hyper speculation only happens in certain places. The market, in general, isn’t the casino it was during the Dot-com bubble and the housing market crash.

One great example is 2022, when the S&P 500 lost roughly a quarter of its value from its peak to the bottom. This happened after the Fed ended QE and started aggressively hiking rates, ending a massive bull market in speculative assets that had worked its way into everything you can imagine, as even pictures of rocks sold for millions.

When the market flushed out these investments, it lost a quarter of its value. The tech-heavy QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) lost roughly 35% of its value. The ultra-high growth ETF (ARKK) lost 80%. Meanwhile, the S&P 500 Value ETF (IVE) lost just 17%.

In other words, bubbles in some parts of the market can be ironed out without major negative consequences for the average investor (who diversifies without piling into every "hot" trade on the market).

Moreover, the market can stomach high valuations as long as earnings growth is high. Using data shared by Bloomberg’s Nir Kaissar, 95% of S&P 500 companies are expected to grow their earnings next year with an average growth rate of 16%, led by the Mag-7 stocks, which are expected to grow by 21%.

In the current earnings season, which is now starting to heat up, FactSet expects 8.0% S&P 500 earnings growth. At the end of June, expectations were for 7.3% growth, which shows that even earnings expectations are moving in the right direction.

Adding to that, while the market may be as expensive as it was during the Dot-com bubble on a forward P/E basis, it is also more profitable and, as I already mentioned, led by companies that are everything except speculative vehicles. Sure, I think they will increasingly compete with each other, but for now, they remain ultra-wide-moat tech giants with strong cash flows to maintain healthy balance sheets and service hundreds of billions in AI-related CapEx. That’s not "bubbly."

To give you an example, in 2023, Alphabet (GOOGL) spent $32 billion on CapEx. Next year, that number is expected to be $94 billion. Meanwhile, it is expected to grow free cash flow (that’s operating cash flow after CapEx) from $69 billion to $83 billion. While this would imply a free cash flow yield of less than 3.0%, it shows just how incredibly powerful these players are. Moreover, these billions all flow downstream into AI suppliers and similar companies.

This is not typical for a bubble unless AI were to suddenly become an obsolete technology.

Assuming It’s Not A Bubble, We Still Need To Adjust

Don’t get me wrong, I am not writing these articles to convince people to keep plowing money into the market. My point is that this isn’t an obvious bubble. The market may show bubbles in some areas, yet overall health remains decent.

However, it doesn’t mean we shouldn’t make adjustments.

To use the words of Nir Kaissar again, if we assume the S&P 500 keeps growing consistently without a further increase in the market’s valuation, we can assume mid-single-digit total returns (earnings growth + dividends), which would imply subdued S&P 500 returns for years to come:

The market’s more modest expectations are very achievable but not necessarily bullish for medium-term returns. If companies deliver what’s expected, and the market rewards them by maintaining their elevated valuations, investors should collect their 5.6% earnings growth in addition to the S&P 500’s dividend yield of 1.2%, for a total return of about 6.8% a year. That’s less than half the S&P 500’s total return over the past decade. - Bloomberg

In other words, technically speaking (based on current estimates), the market is not in a bubble, yet the outlook now shows we should expect future total returns to be half of what they have been in recent years.

This has been my thesis for a while, as most of my readers may know, which means I have been preparing for a while.

  • I hold no market ETFs. Instead, I focus on individual stocks, which allows me to find value without being forced to buy the entire basket.
  • Even in ETF-heavy accounts like those of my parents, we have started to focus more on individual stock picks and value ETFs like diversified dividend ETFs.
  • I believe the equal-weight S&P 500 will beat the market-weighted S&P 500 due to valuation benefits and potentially broadening economic growth, as AI hyperscaler spending works its way through various supply chains.

Now, to give you some examples, I am not applying rocket science in the asset allocation process. I am keeping it as simple as possible and doing two key things:

  • I look for areas with secular growth tailwinds and/or potential bottoms.
  • I then pick the stocks that fit my view.

To avoid giving you the same picks every time, I decided to provide some more food for thought. One company I like is Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), which owns the NYSE exchange and has spent more than a decade aggressively diversifying its business into areas like mortgage services.

Now, it has a business that thrives on higher volatility and a potential rebound in housing with more than 50% recurring revenues and an adjusted operating margin of 60%. Not even the Great Financial Crisis caused its EPS to dip.

Since going public roughly 20 years ago, it has returned more than 17.0% per year. Currently, it trades a bit above 23.0x earnings, a point below its long-term average. When adding 11-13% expected annual EPS growth, I think this opportunity is terrific.

To me, ICE combines energy due to its massive energy futures portfolio, housing thanks to its mortgage expansion, and general volatility benefits from its volume-based business, as I expect "higher for longer" to translate to both higher inflation and higher market volatility.

Another company I really like is Watsco (WSO). Watsco currently yields more than 3.0% and is a leader in the HVAC market, where it acts as a distributor that connects actual manufacturers to customers like HVAC technicians. This makes it a housing player with a smart business model that is easy to scale and has secular growth in climate solutions.

The reason I did not buy Watsco is that I preferred Comfort Systems USA (FIX), which is now my biggest holding and an engineering solutions company with a focus on industrial (i.e., data center) HVAC systems. Watsco is mainly residential, which means its stock price is under pressure from elevated rates and a housing market that’s stuck.

Fortunately, this has pushed its P/E ratio down to its 10-year average. Now, analysts expect a rebound in EPS to start in 2026, which would align with my view of a much bigger focus on housing, as I wrote in a recent article.

These are just two examples, but they show you what kind of investments I’m looking for in this market, as I believe there’s still plenty of value to be found in areas like housing, stock exchanges, general cyclical value, and energy, which may be my favorite spot to invest.

For now, it all boils down to this:

Takeaway

I dislike this market. I have often said that.

However, I don’t think it’s a bubble. While valuations are elevated, they are also supported by serious earnings power, durable secular growth like the AI growth cycle, while some of the crazy high-flying stocks are still too small to create systemic risks.

That said, it doesn’t matter that we should all blindly pump money into this market.

The reason I’m writing this article is to both discuss bubble allegations and to make clear that even if this isn’t a bubble, we should still expect subdued total returns going forward, which makes diversification critical.

By diversifying and finding undervalued opportunities in areas with secular tailwinds, we can substantially improve our risk/reward and potentially achieve market-beating returns when it matters most, which is in tricky situations like the current one.

Risks To My Thesis

Needless to say, as my thesis is that there is no AI bubble, the risk is that I’m wrong. I could be wrong if it turns out that AI adoption is much slower than anticipated, which could end up pressuring Big Tech returns and even slow AI spending. That would have substantial negative consequences for growth, as the market has started to rely on AI spending in light of weak cyclical growth.

Other risks are general risks of unforeseen developments, like an escalating trade war or anything that could question the durability of S&P 500 earnings growth expectations.

data.title: TSMC: Understanding The Golden Goose Of AI
data.description:
Investment Thesis

After writing many articles about different big tech companies and after looking carefully at the market sector with multiples too expensive for my taste, I think it's crucial to focus on the company that I consider to be the heart of the AI industry: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM).

Besides focusing on the still attractive price of its shares, and in the recent results of Q3, I'd like to explain why it's the central piece of the supply chain that supplies the world's most advanced technology sector with its silicon chips. I'll try to do it in a simple way and, because I'm not an engineer, and I'll try to focus on what we like most: the charts with market shares.

The market share is too high, added to the underlying demand that will continue to exist thanks to the AI boom, which leads me to think that at the current prices, the shares are still very cheap, representing a great buying opportunity as a short- and medium term investment.

Q3: A Demand That Continues To Grow

Q3 delivered fantastic results, in my opinion, with YoY revenue growth of 40% to $33.1 billion, exceeding the market's expected ceiling of $33 billion. Another metric that demonstrates the potential for the company's strong demand for chips is the gross margin, which was 59.5%, above the market's expected ceiling of 57.5%.

Meanwhile, demand for the company's largest segment, HPC (High Performance Computing), which makes the most significant contribution to training and running AI models, remained stable year-over-year, representing 57% of revenue. However, the non-AI end-market segment is showing a slight recovery, according to CEO C.C. Wei's comments on the conference call. The other sectors of Smartphone, IoT and Automotive showed growth of between 18 and 20%.

Wei also provided details of the company's capital expenditures as part of the relocation of its factories and its significant investments to address the structural demand for AI. Around 70% of the company's nearly $42 billion in capex is allocated to advanced process technologies. Details were also provided on new 2-nanometer chip products. Let's start with this, which I believe represents another strong catalyst for the future.

Our 2-nanometer and A16 technologies lead the industry in addressing sizable demand for energy-efficient computing, and almost all innovators are working with TSMC. N2 is well on track for volume production later this quarter. With full year, we expect a faster ramp in 2026, fueled by both smartphone and HPC AI applications.

With our strategy of continuous enhancement, we also introduced N2P as an extension of our N2 family. N2P feature further performance and power benefits on top of N2 and volume production is scheduled for second half '26.

We also introduced A16 featuring our best-in-class Super Power Rail or SPR. A16 is best suited for specific HPC product with compressed signal routes and dense power delivery networks. Volume production is on track for the second half '26. We believe N2, N2P, A16 and its derivatives will propel our N2 family to be another large and long-lasting node for TSMC.

Finally, I believe the company's weight outside of Taiwan is an important factor to consider. This is the growth of the US market, which accounted for 76% of revenue in Q3, while China accounted for 8%. If we look at the following Seeking Alpha chart, the trend continues to lean toward the United States, even though similar figures have been seen in the past. Since Q3 2024, I see the trend continuing. I consider this important because I will comment on it in the risks section.

The Heart Of AI

TSM represented 71% of the chip foundry market during Q2 2025, surpassing its main competitors such as Samsung, SMIC, and UMC.

But let's start from the beginning. For hyperscalers like Google, Meta, Microsoft and Amazon (or the world's leading startup like OpenAI) to be able to meet their customers' demand for adopting the most advanced generative AI in their businesses globally, they need millions of microchips. These microchips are designed by big semiconductors companies such as Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Broadcom (AVGO), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Nvidia (NVDA), of course, the world's biggest company in market capitalization, among others.

Note that I've talked about those that design the microchips, but not about those who manufacture them. Here comes TSM into scene. The Taiwanese company manufactures, based on its silicon wafers, those microchips designed by the big semiconductors. Let's highlight something else, that's better shown in the following chart that I've done based on a Gartner report: the market share among those who design the microchips is much more diversified.

Nvidia is the main player in the demand for its GPUs (it owns 94% of that market), and the advanced Blackrowell products and H20, which are the main options for AI workloads in data centers. But although it is the main player, it owns just 11% of the market share, while TSM, in the foundry business, has 71%. Behind NVDA are Samsung and Intel, which are also in the foundry business, and that also design their own chips.

The manufacturing of the smallest chips, from 7 nanometers to 3 nanometers, represents 74% of its income. Technically, the smaller the number of nanometers, the more transistors can be manufactured in the same physical space, creating more powerful chips with a lower energy consumption. The smaller the chips, the more they attract the demand of the AI data center. This is easily observed in the growth of the income, because the 3nm and 5nm represented the higher increase. And the greatest thing is yet to come with 2nm, as I've mentioned before.

So, once we understand TSM's comparative advantage and foundry leadership, I'd like to show another part of the supply chain, which I also focused on in writing this article, which is the one behind TSM: the silicon wafer industry and the EUV lithography machinery industry.

The silicon wafer industry presents a competitive landscape, although according to a Mordor Intelligence report, just three major players hold 70% of the market: Shin-Etsu Chemical, SUMCO, and GlobalWafers Source, but other companies such as GlobalFoundries (GFS) and Intel are also vying for market share.

Finally, in the lithography market, competition is less fierce and there are fewer major players, with only one major player monopolizing the majority of the market by offering EUV machines: the Dutch company ASML Holding (ASML). ASML's strength, as detailed in a related article by author Rick Orford, is that it is the only one offering EUV machines, which are those that print the patterns on the silicon wafer, and its position is almost monopolistic in this regard, although not in the entire lithography market.

However, some risks persist related to its own supply chains, the high maintenance costs of EUV machinery, and geopolitical competition from China, both in controlling the export of its rare earths and in its quest to achieve its own manufacturing of lithography equipment. Incidentally, there is already a Chinese company pursuing this goal: Shanghai Microelectronics Equipment. And although it is still far from achieving it, I believe AMSL bulls should at least mention it.

With this brief overview of the supply chain that feeds semiconductors, and in turn, Big Tech (and in turn, the AI ​​craze) I think we can now understand TSM's place. Above its value chain are the big semiconductor players competing with each other, while one step higher are the hyperscalers offering the most innovative service of our century (the AI) while spending vast amounts of money above their FCF. And so it seems it will continue, at least in the short term.

Below TSM's supply chain are the wafer manufacturers, which, as we saw, show an interesting diversification. Further down the line are the chip manufacturing machine suppliers, of which, as we saw, ASML is the undisputed leader in its sector. However, ASML trades at higher multiples than TSM. ASML's EV/EBITDA and P/E are 26.6x and 34.9x, respectively, while TSM's EV/EBITDA and P/E are 14.8x and 24.3x, respectively. I consider TSM's work irreplaceable because, at the moment, no company can match its scale of cutting-edge chip manufacturing.

Valuation

TSM's stock price, given its growth potential and strategic market position, strikes me as incredibly cheap. We can see this in its P/E of 25x versus the industry median of 30x, while we can also see, in terms of the last 10 years, how it is still far from its 35x ceiling in 2021.

To properly assess the company’s internal potential looking into the future, I developed two DCFs based on conservative estimates of its EBIT growth, which was $40 billion in 2024 and could reach $60 billion in 2025 if the current growth rate is maintained, which, if we look at the first 9 months of the year was approximately $45 billion. What’s more, in the last 5 quarters, EBIT grew more than 42%, and I show this in the following graph, although without incorporating the EBIT growth in the third quarter of 2025.

I then started my first model with an EBIT of $60 billion and applied a 5-year CAGR similar to the last 3 years of approximately 25%. I consider this important because Seeking Alpha's 5-year CAGR estimate is 32%, while in the last 5 or so quarters, EBIT has grown between 40% and 50%. But again, my intention is to be conservative to show the true potential.

The other metrics I use to complete my models are a tax rate of 11.9% and a WACC of 8.1%, as estimated by Value Investing, and I apply a long-term growth rate of 3.5%, slightly above the global average, given TSM's strong position in its sector. I'm using 5.2 billion shares outstanding, not the total number of shares, which amounts to 25.9 billion, as this is the figure you will find in Seeking Alpha's information, but since this is a foreign stock, and as reported by the company in its 20-F, slide 184, about each ADS (American Depositary Shares) representing 5 common shares, I am using 5.2 billion.

The upside here is 31.9% with a $402 price target. I emphasize that I'm being conservative. If we're going for a 32% EBIT CAGR (which is SA's projection), the upside is around 86.7%, with a $569 price target.

Risks

Regarding risks, it's worth clarifying that there are two important underlying issues related not so much to its business model but to completely external issues: geopolitics. On the one hand, Taiwan lives under constant threat of invasion by China, and this generates a logical commotion among investors. Although I consider an invasion unlikely given the high military and political costs that China would pay, the indirect impacts that China could have on TSM (and, failing that, on Taiwan, since its market cap exceeds the island's GDP) are more likely. One such measure that could harm the company is, for example, China's restriction on rare earth exports.

TSM is advancing a geographic diversification plan to reduce its regional risks, for example, investing $165 billion in the United States to operate its own factory in Phoenix, Arizona. While TSM's ties to the United States are important, in my opinion, there is another risk for the company: that the "America First" policy will end up undermining the "Silicon Shield" policy.

If the United States successfully moves forward with its effort to relocate chip manufacturing away from Taiwan, it could remove the island's deterrent claim to being the world's leading semiconductor manufacturer. Howard Lutnick, Trump's Commerce Secretary, said they are seeking an optimal 50-50 distribution.

[The production concentration] is not healthy for you [Taiwan] or healthy for us [the United States] because we protect you, and for us to protect you, you need to help us achieve reasonable self-sufficiency. … Let’s get to 50-50. We’re producing half and you’re producing half. We’re still fundamentally reliant on you because we can’t live without the other half. But if we have half, we have the capacity to do what we need to do, if we need to do it.

In short, this isn't a unique risk for TSM, but rather a broader geopolitical risk that threatens the entire semiconductor industry. While these risks remain mitigated, the company may experience fluctuations or even slow down processes, but its leadership in chip manufacturing, for the time being, is irreplaceable, in my opinion. At current prices, and with Q3 results, the buy option remains an attractive medium-term opportunity.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

data.title: FIDU: Industrials Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series reports subsector metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the industrials sector. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI) and Fidelity MSCI Industrials Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FIDU), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italics describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or unavailable when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for transportation in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in transportation companies. The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). In the same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.
